Title: Acyl-CoA:diacylglycerol acyltransferase: Molecular biology, biochemistry and biotechnology

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Final acylation step of the sn-glycerol-3-phosphate/Kennedy pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acyl group from acyl-CoA to sn-1,2-diacylglycerol to form triacylglycerol
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point/important target for oil accumulation engineering; rate-limiting status not universally established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generally promotes TG synthesis and storage; overexpression often increases seed oil/TG content, while loss of function reduces oil in some systems
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast/insect/mammalian cells; knockout mutants; overexpression in plants; in vitro enzyme assays; metabolite profiling; field trials in crops
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Varies by species; in mammals broadly expressed with high levels in small intestine and adipose tissue; in plants commonly high during seed development and also detected in flowers/pollen or other tissues
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum; predominantly ER in animals; distinct ER regions in plants; not typically lipid-droplet associated in mammals
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: MBOAT family membrane-bound O-acyltransferase; multiple transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Acyl-CoA binding protein (ACBP); stearoyl-CoA desaturase 1 (context of DGAT complexes discussed mainly for DGAT2); DGAT1 self-association/homotetramer
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy storage reservoir; source of signaling molecules; substrate reserve for membrane biogenesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed embryos/endosperm; mesocarp in olive; adipose tissue; liver; intestine-associated storage pools; lipid droplets
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High-fat diet; oleate stimulation; drought in canola field studies; diet-induced obesity models
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In mice, knockout reduces lactation and tissue TG; engineering may alter fatty acid composition or seed traits; detailed tradeoffs vary by species
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DGAT1 is one of the two major membrane-bound DGAT families catalyzing the terminal acylation step to TG. 2. Genetic and transgenic evidence shows DGAT1 strongly influences seed oil accumulation in several crops and tissue TG metabolism in animals. 3. DGAT1 often has broader acyl acceptor range than DGAT2 and can show additional acyltransferase activities in some mammals. 4. DGAT1 is an important biotechnology target for oilseed engineering and an anti-obesity therapeutic target in mammals.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for increasing seed oil content in crops via overexpression; useful in stacking with unusual fatty acid biosynthesis genes; mammalian DGAT1 is a drug target for obesity, hypertriglyceridemia and steatosis, requiring tissue-specific safety consideration
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genomics and bioinformatics surveys mentioned; no unified accession dataset for the review; individual gene IDs summarized in tables
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review summarizing orthologs from plants, animals, fungi and protists. Functional details, topology and regulation can differ substantially among orthologs, so species-specific interpretation is necessary.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Final acylation step of the sn-glycerol-3-phosphate/Kennedy pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acyl group from acyl-CoA to sn-1,2-diacylglycerol to form triacylglycerol
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a major determinant of TG synthesis in many contexts, especially mammalian and unusual-fatty-acid systems; formal universal rate limitation not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes TG synthesis; in mammals often makes a predominant contribution to TG biosynthesis, and in some plants preferentially channels unusual fatty acids into TG
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification from fungus; heterologous expression in yeast/insect/mammalian cells; knockout and antisense studies in mice; plant transgenics; in vitro enzyme assays; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Varies by species; in humans highly expressed in hepatocytes and adipocytes; in mice broader tissue distribution; in plants often enriched in developing seeds of unusual-oil species such as castor and tung
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum; ER-lipid droplet interface; lipid droplets; mitochondria-associated membranes in animals
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DGAT2 family acyltransferase; fewer transmembrane domains than DGAT1; conserved HPHG/EPHS motif
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Stearoyl-CoA desaturase 1; DGAT2 self-association/oligomerization; potential protein interactions proposed in plant systems
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy storage reservoir; source of signaling molecules; substrate reserve for membrane biogenesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed oil bodies/lipid droplets; adipose tissue; liver; cytosolic lipid droplets
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High-fat diet; oleate stimulation; dietary fat; antisense oligonucleotide treatment; unusual-fatty-acid production contexts
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complete knockout in mice causes lipopenia, skin abnormalities and early death; engineering unusual-fatty-acid flux may alter oil composition and physiology
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DGAT2 is a distinct non-homologous DGAT family that catalyzes the same terminal TG-forming reaction as DGAT1. 2. In mammals DGAT2 often plays a predominant role in TG biosynthesis and is essential for normal physiology. 3. In several unusual-oil plants, DGAT2 preferentially incorporates unusual fatty acids such as ricinoleic, α-eleostearic and vernolic acids into TG. 4. DGAT2 is a major target for both therapeutic modulation of hepatic TG and engineering of specialty oils.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for boosting oil content and especially for channeling unusual fatty acids into seed oils through gene stacking with fatty acid-modifying enzymes; therapeutic inhibition may reduce hepatic steatosis but requires caution because complete loss is deleterious
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genomics and bioinformatics surveys mentioned; no unified accession dataset for the review; individual gene IDs summarized in tables
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Role is species dependent: dispensable for Arabidopsis seed oil accumulation but important in castor and tung; topology and motif function differ among animals, fungi and plants.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Monoacylglycerol/diacylglycerol-dependent neutral lipid biosynthesis related to triacylglycerol metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: DGAT2-related branch linking monoacylglycerol acylation to DG and TG formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Primarily acylate monoacylglycerol to diacylglycerol and, when overexpressed, also display DGAT activity that converts diacylglycerol to triacylglycerol
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Monoacylglycerol; acyl-CoA; also diacylglycerol in DGAT side activity assays
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and can also directly form target product via DGAT activity
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream to terminal TG step, with auxiliary terminal activity
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: MGAT2 supported as a key control point in dietary fat absorption and metabolic disease response; others not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contribute to intestinal fat absorption and broader lipid metabolism; MGAT2 deficiency protects against high-fat-diet-induced metabolic disorders
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression in mammalian/insect cells; knockout mice; tissue expression analyses; in vitro enzyme assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: MGAT1 high in stomach and kidney, not intestine; MGAT2 predominant in small intestine and also human liver; MGAT3 in human gastrointestinal tract, especially ileum, and also liver
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy storage and dietary fat utilization
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Enterocytes and systemic lipid storage tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High-fat diet; dietary fat absorption conditions
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Inhibition or knockout can alter intestinal fat absorption; useful metabolically but may affect nutrient handling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MGAT genes are DGAT2-related enzymes primarily involved in monoacylglycerol acylation during dietary fat absorption. 2. All three mammalian MGAT isoforms can also show DGAT activity when overexpressed. 3. MGAT2 is particularly important for energy metabolism under high-fat diet conditions and is a therapeutic target.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primarily relevant as therapeutic targets for metabolic disease rather than crop engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in the review as DGAT-related enzymes rather than canonical major DGAT families.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes synthesis of wax esters and also acylates diacylglycerol to form triacylglycerol; some also show MGAT activity
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal step in bacterial TG branch
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Bacteria; yeast heterologous systems; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes TG and/or wax ester synthesis in bacteria and heterologous systems
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; heterologous expression in yeast or E. coli; in vitro activity assays
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bifunctional WS/DGAT family unrelated to eukaryotic DGAT1/DGAT2
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Storage lipid and wax lipid formation; persistence traits in some bacteria
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Bacterial neutral lipid bodies/cellular lipid stores
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Growth-limiting conditions in Acinetobacter calcoaceticus ADP1
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Bacterial WS/DGAT enzymes are unrelated to eukaryotic DGAT1/2 but can synthesize TG. 2. They are bifunctional enzymes that also make wax esters and sometimes show MGAT activity. 3. Heterologous expression can enhance TG production in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for synthetic biology and industrial lipid engineering where wax ester/TG co-production is desired
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The Arabidopsis WS/DGAT ortholog predominantly synthesizes wax esters rather than acting as a major TG synthase.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acylates diacylglycerol to form triacylglycerol as a soluble DGAT-related enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal step in soluble TG synthesis route
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arachis hypogaea and homologs in other plants
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Peanut and recombinant E. coli; inferred homologs in Arabidopsis, soybean, rice
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports soluble/cytosolic TG synthesis, indicating TG formation is not exclusively membrane-bound
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Purification from developing cotyledons; recombinant expression in E. coli; sequence comparison; enzymatic activity assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in developing peanut cotyledons; Arabidopsis putative DGAT3 transcript increases during seed maturation based on computational analysis
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Soluble DGAT-related protein with limited similarity to bacterial WS/DGAT
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Cross-reactivity with Rhodotorula glutinis 10S multi-enzyme complex DGAT peptide
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Developing seed cotyledons; cytosolic lipid pools
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DGAT3 is a soluble plant DGAT-related enzyme identified from peanut. 2. Its existence shows that TG synthesis can occur outside membranes. 3. Homologs are present in other plants, but their in vivo contribution requires further study.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential alternative engineering target for cytosolic TG accumulation, but currently less validated than DGAT1/2
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray computational analysis mentioned for Arabidopsis putative DGAT3
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes this enzyme class is less understood and needs validation of expression-activity relationships.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: BAHD acyltransferase-mediated soluble TG synthesis linked to cutin precursor production
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes synthesis of triacylglycerol enriched in hydroxy fatty acids that may serve as precursors for cutin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product and supplies precursor for cutin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes synthesis of hydroxy-FA-rich TG implicated in cutin precursor formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene characterization; heterologous expression in yeast; biochemical functional assays
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely affects cuticle biology, but specific tradeoffs not summarized here
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis DCR is a BAHD acyltransferase with DGAT-related function. 2. It synthesizes hydroxy-FA-rich TG linked to cutin formation rather than bulk storage oil accumulation. 3. This expands the mechanistic diversity of DGAT-related enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential niche target for modifying cutin-related lipid metabolism rather than mainstream oil yield engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is a DGAT-related soluble acyltransferase, not a canonical DGAT1/2 enzyme.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Wax ester and neutral lipid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Predominantly synthesize wax esters and also exhibit DGAT, MGAT and acyl-CoA:retinol acyltransferase activities
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Wax alcohol; acyl-CoA; also monoacylglycerol/diacylglycerol in side-activity assays
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Wax esters; triacylglycerol; diacylglycerol; retinyl esters
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product in side activity but mainly acts in parallel lipid branch
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal step in wax ester branch with auxiliary TG activity
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Human; recombinant yeast and insect cell systems
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contribute to skin lipid metabolism, predominantly wax ester synthesis, with additional DGAT-related activity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; heterologous overexpression; enzyme activity assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Predominant in skin; AWAT2 transcripts restricted in sebaceous glands
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Skin surface lipid formation and barrier-related functions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AWAT1 and AWAT2 are DGAT2-related enzymes in mammals. 2. They mainly synthesize wax esters in skin but can also exhibit DGAT activity. 3. They illustrate diversification of DGAT2-like acyltransferases into tissue-specialized lipid functions.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant mainly for mammalian skin lipid biology and potential dermatological applications
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These are not core bulk TG enzymes but DGAT-related multifunctional acyltransferases included in the review.
